gazette: Ministry of Trade, Technical Barriers Information and Notification Center
title: The maximum residue levels for phosmet have been revised
bodyHtml:
In a letter received from the Permanent Representation of Turkey to the European Union, it is stated that a decision has been made not to renew the approval of the active substance "phosmet" under Regulation (EU) 2022/94 of the Commission, published on January 25, 2022. It is mentioned that phosmet is an active substance used in various products, particularly in insecticides and acaricides applied to fruits such as citrus, apple, and peach. It is widely used in the treatment of citrus fruit pests, fruit flies, and citrus psyllids. The letter also states that the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) opinion published on July 20, 2022, regarding the review of maximum residue levels (MRLs) for phosmet, identified a risk for consumers concerning MRLs for grapefruit, orange, lemon, lime, tangerine, coconut, apple, pear, quince, medlar, feijoa, apricot, peach, table grape, wine grape, blueberry, cranberry, kumquat, and potato.

Furthermore, due to data deficiencies, EFSA acknowledged that it could not disregard the harmful effects of current MRLs for phosmet on human health for all products. Therefore, it was confirmed that none of the current MRLs for phosmet, including those based on Codex maximum residue limits, have been confirmed as safe for consumers. In this context, an example published in the Official Journal of the European Union, dated May 26, 2023, and numbered L 139, is attached to the mentioned letter. According to Regulation (EU) 2023/1029 of the Commission, amending Annexes III and V of Regulation (EC) No 396/2005 of the European Parliament and of the Council, published on May 25, 2023, in terms of maximum residue levels for phosmet in certain products, the MRLs for phosmet have been reduced to the limit of quantification, which is the lowest level detectable by the most modern and reliable analytical methods. Under the Regulation, MRLs for phosmet have been established as 0.01 mg/kg for most fruits and 0.005 mg/kg for oranges, apples, and pears, differing from the MRLs determined by the Codex Alimentarius. It is stated that the new MRLs under the Regulation will be applicable from September 15, 2023. However, it is also noted that if food products exported to the EU are placed on the EU market after September 15, 2023, compliance with the new MRLs will be required.
